summary: This notebook demonstrates a baseline pipeline for predicting multiple polymer properties (Tg, FFV, Tc, Density, Rg) using tabular molecular features. It emphasizes robust SMILES cleaning and extensive integration of external datasets to augment the training set. The author extracts combined RDKit fingerprints and descriptors, supplements them with graph-based molecular features, and applies target-specific feature selection. Data scarcity is addressed via SMILES randomization and Gaussian Mixture Model augmentation. Separate XGBoost regressors are trained per target with Optuna-tuned hyperparameters, highlighting that careful data curation and feature engineering are critical for this chemistry prediction task.
competition_flow: Loads competition and external CSV/XLSX data, cleans and validates SMILES, integrates external targets, extracts molecular fingerprints and descriptors, applies variance filtering and augmentation, trains separate XGBoost regressors per target, and outputs a CSV submission.
data_reading: Uses pandas read_csv and read_excel to load competition train/test files and multiple external datasets from /kaggle/input/ paths.
data_processing: Canonicalizes SMILES, aggressively filters invalid R-group and bracket patterns, removes rows with missing targets, averages duplicate SMILES in external data, converts units (e.g., Kelvin to Celsius, density offset), splits data 80/20 for validation, and applies StandardScaler normalization.
features_engineering: Morgan fingerprints (radius=2, 128 bits), MACCS keys, RDKit descriptors (MolWt, LogP, TPSA, RotatableBonds, NumAtoms, etc.), graph-based features (diameter, average shortest path, cycle count via networkx), Optuna-selected descriptor subsets per target, VarianceThreshold filtering, SMILES randomization, and GMM-based synthetic sample generation.
insights: External data integration significantly increases sample availability per target.
SMILES randomization and GMM augmentation effectively mitigate data scarcity.
Graph-based molecular descriptors complement traditional RDKit features for capturing topology.
Target-specific feature subsets and Optuna-tuned hyperparameters improve baseline regression performance.
critical_findings: R-group notation and bracketed patterns in SMILES consistently break RDKit parsers and must be strictly filtered.
External datasets require careful unit conversion and column mapping to align with competition targets.
Several RDKit descriptor functions fail silently on certain molecules and require explicit try/except handling.
